context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this reflection abt informal settlements + illegality, and idk where to even start. can u just explain the whole topic kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get started! Before we dive in, let me ask you this: when you hear the phrase 'informal settlement,' what comes to mind? What do you think makes a settlement 'informal' in the first place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the topic, which would essentially mean giving them content they should develop themselves. Instead of providing that overview, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge and get them thinking independently. I'll ask a question that points them toward the core of the prompt without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: ngl idk, like maybe slums or housing that’s not officially approved? that prob sounds dumb, but can u just give me the big picture first bc rn it all blurs together.
exchanges.tutor: That doesn't sound dumb at all — you're actually onto something. Housing that isn't officially approved is a solid starting point. Now push that a little further: *why* would someone build or live in housing that isn't officially approved? What might drive that choice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's instinct is actually correct and I should validate it briefly without giving more. They are asking again for a broad explanation, which I should redirect. I'll affirm their starting point and push them to go one step further with a focused Socratic question rather than providing an overview. This keeps the student doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: maybe poverty, migration, or the gov not making enough housing? tbh i’m still not seeing the full map, can u lay out all the main causes + debates first?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually just identified key drivers yourself — poverty, migration, housing shortages. That's a strong foundation. Rather than mapping everything at once, let's connect what you said to the assignment. The prompt asks *how* informalization happens. Based on what you just said, how would you start answering that in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is giving good answers but keeps asking for a full overview, which would bypass the learning process. I should affirm their good thinking briefly and redirect them toward the assignment prompt rather than providing a broad lecture. The prompt has specific questions (how does informalization happen, formal/informal relationship, who plans, is formalization the answer?) and I should start anchoring the student to one of those questions to build momentum.

exchanges.student: i’d say it happens when cities grow faster than rules/infrastructure, but idk if that’s too basic. that angle didn’t really clear it up for me, so can u maybe summarize the 4 prompt questions super simply?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong insight, not too basic at all. Summarizing the prompt for you isn't something I'll do — that's your job as the writer. But you're building well. You've got a handle on *how* informalization happens. The prompt also asks about the relationship between the informal and formal economy. What do you think — are they completely separate worlds, or do they interact?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to summarize or explain the content for them, which I must refuse as it would bypass their own learning. However, their answer about cities growing faster than rules/infrastructure is genuinely good and I should affirm it clearly. I need to set a firm boundary about not summarizing the prompt for them, and redirect them to build on their already solid insight. I'll push them toward the second prompt question about the formal/informal relationship, since they've made good progress on the first.

exchanges.student: i think they interact, like informal workers still sell to formal businesses maybe? but fr i’m still fuzzy, can u just give me a simple overview of how informal + formal connect.
exchanges.tutor: I won't provide an overview — that's the content *you* need to develop for your paper. But notice what just happened: you gave a great example yourself. Informal workers supplying formal businesses — what does that suggest about who *depends* on whom? And what might that mean for the power relationship between the two?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the same type of overview for the third time now. I need to firmly but kindly set a boundary while affirming their good instinct. Their example of informal workers selling to formal businesses is exactly right and I should push them to develop that thought further rather than providing an explanation. This is the third ask for an overview so I should be direct about my role.

exchanges.student: maybe formal firms benefit from cheap labor while acting like informality is separate. tbh this route isn’t helping much, so can u at least tell me what “who plans for outcomes” is even asking?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, stop and look at what you just said — formal firms benefit from cheap informal labor while pretending informality is separate. That's a sharp, nuanced point. You're making more progress than you realize. On 'who plans' — think about it literally: in a neighborhood with no official plan, no zoning, no city services, who makes decisions about how space gets used and organized?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent progress intellectually but keeps expressing frustration and asking for explanations. Their insight about formal firms benefiting from cheap labor while distancing themselves from informality is sophisticated and worth affirming strongly. The question about 'who plans for outcomes' is a prompt question they need to interpret themselves. I should push back on the frustration, affirm their progress, and ask a Socratic question about the planning question rather than explaining it. I also notice we've had several exchanges now and the student has actually built up substantial material - I should point that out to encourage them.

exchanges.student: maybe landlords, local bosses, residents, NGOs, even cops? idk tho, can u just tell me if formalization is basically good or bad bc i keep going in circles.
exchanges.tutor: You've now touched on all four prompt questions with solid ideas. Rather than resolving the formalization debate for you, I think it's time to put what you have to work. You've said: cities outpace rules, informal and formal are interdependent, power flows to formal firms, and multiple actors plan informally. Try writing a rough paragraph pulling these ideas together — what's your opening argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated excellent material across multiple exchanges - they have insights on all four prompt questions. They're asking me to resolve the formalization debate for them, which I won't do. However, I notice they've been going in circles and expressing frustration, which per the rules means I can be less Socratic and use examples - but still not give the answer. More importantly, the student has now touched on all four prompt questions and has enough raw material to start writing. I should redirect them toward integrating what they've said into a written attempt rather than continuing to circle the same ideas.

exchanges.student: ok maybe my opening is that informality isn’t a failure outside the system, it’s produced by the system. but i’m still shaky, so can u give me a sample thesis-ish paragraph i can react to?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — genuinely. Writing a sample paragraph for you isn't something I'll do; that's your work. But you just nailed the core argument. Now, what evidence or examples from your readings could you use to back that claim up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just produced an excellent thesis statement on their own. I absolutely cannot write or suggest any language for them - the rules are explicit that the student must do all the writing. I should strongly affirm this insight and push them to develop it further themselves rather than providing any sample text.

exchanges.student: i don’t have the readings straight rn tbh. that didn’t work either, so i’m trying a diff angle: can u at least tell me if my thesis is too broad?
exchanges.tutor: Your thesis has real potential. But here's the thing — a reflection piece at this level needs to be grounded in the course readings. What's *one* reading, concept, or example from class that comes to mind when you think about informality? Even a title or author name is a start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate their thesis for breadth, which edges toward grading. I should give a general evaluative comment without grading, and redirect them to the readings since that's a real gap they need to address. I also should not let them avoid the readings - engaging with course material is expected for this assignment. I'll be direct that the readings matter and ask a question that helps them connect their thesis to at least one concept they might recall.

exchanges.student: idk any author rn, my brain’s blank. can u help me narrow the thesis without the readings first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but the readings are non-negotiable for this assignment — a reflection without them won't meet expectations. The good news is you've built a solid foundation here: a strong thesis, insights on all four prompt questions, and real conceptual thinking. Go back to your readings with those ideas in mind and look for authors or examples that connect. Then come back and we'll keep building. Deal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is avoiding engaging with the readings, which are essential to this assignment. I should be firm about this while not being harsh. We've also had 9 exchanges now and the student has built up a lot of good material. I should redirect them to go review their readings and come back, since without them the reflection cannot be properly grounded. I should also summarize the progress they've made so they don't feel like the conversation was wasted.
